---
title: "A tier — core homes for semiparametric and causal work"
---

These journals are where most of the methodological work in our subfield actually lives. For a CTML-affiliated researcher, you should expect to place many of your career papers here.

## Biometrics

**What it is:** The flagship journal of the International Biometric Society. The natural home for biostatistical methodology.

**Focus:** Methodology with clear biological or medical applications. Survival analysis, longitudinal data, clinical trials, causal inference in health settings, genomics methodology.

**Representative work:**

- Many mediation analysis methodology papers (VanderWeele, Tchetgen Tchetgen)
- TMLE applications in observational health data
- Survival analysis methodology

**Why it lands here and not S:** The bar is high but the audience is explicitly biostatistics rather than general statistics. A paper that is well-suited to Biometrics might be out of scope at JRSS-B.

## Biostatistics

**What it is:** The Oxford-published biostatistics journal. Somewhat more theoretical in flavor than *Biometrics*; smaller page count.

**Focus:** Methods for biomedical applications, with an emphasis on genomics, imaging, and complex observational data. Welcoming to semiparametric and causal methodology.

**Representative work:**

- Extensive literature on longitudinal causal inference methodology
- High-dimensional methods for genomics with inferential guarantees
- Many of Hernán and collaborators' methodology papers

## JASA Applications & Case Studies

**What it is:** The A&CS section of JASA. Different editorial track from T&M.

**Focus:** Applied statistics with methodological contribution, or methodology developed in service of a substantive application. Papers here typically tell a strong data story.

**Representative work for our field:** Applied causal inference papers with methodological novelty — evaluating a real policy or treatment effect with a new estimator.

**When to target this vs. T&M:** If the paper's primary contribution is the substantive answer rather than the method, A&CS is the better fit. If the method is the contribution, T&M.

## Epidemiology

**What it is:** The flagship methodological epidemiology journal. Short paper format.

**Focus:** Methodological and conceptual papers in epidemiology, with a heavy emphasis on causal inference.

**Why this matters for us:** A remarkable amount of the *conceptual* foundation of modern causal inference lives here — Hernán, Robins, VanderWeele, Greenland, and others publish foundational work in *Epidemiology* that then gets formalized elsewhere. Target trial emulation, the concept of the g-formula applied to policy, and much of the practical apparatus of causal inference entered the literature here.

**Representative work:**

- Hernán & Robins' target trial emulation papers
- VanderWeele's mediation decomposition papers

**Implicit editorial signal:** *"A clean conceptual contribution or an identification argument that clarifies something."*

## Journal of Econometrics

**What it is:** The leading econometrics journal.

**Focus:** Econometric theory and methodology. Strong on semiparametric efficiency, instrumental variables, moment-based estimation, and debiased ML.

**Why this matters for us:** The econometrics literature has developed a parallel and often more technically developed treatment of semiparametric inference. Chernozhukov and collaborators' work on DML lives here. If your paper has an IV or policy-evaluation flavor, this can be the best audience — econometricians will cite it.

## Journal of Machine Learning Research (JMLR)

**What it is:** Open-access flagship ML journal. Peer-reviewed, longer-form than conference papers.

**Focus:** Machine learning theory and methodology, including a growing body of causal ML work.

**Why it is in A tier:** Much of the modern causal-ML literature (heterogeneous treatment effects, policy learning, conformal prediction for causal quantities) lives here. The editorial standards are genuinely high.

**Caveat for stats departments:** Some classical statistics departments still discount ML venues for tenure. This has shifted substantially in the last decade, especially at methodologically pluralistic places like Berkeley, but worth being aware of.

## Econometrica

**What it is:** The top general econometrics/economics theory journal.

**Focus:** Foundational theoretical contributions in econometrics and economic theory.

**Why it is in A rather than S for our field:** Landing here is extremely hard and a major signal, but the readership is economists, and papers there are read through that lens. If your paper fits the econometrics frame (identification, moment conditions, asymptotic theory of estimators in economic models), it belongs here. For a biostatistician, it is a reach venue rather than a natural home.

## International Journal of Biostatistics (IJB)

**What it is:** De Gruyter-published journal founded with strong Berkeley involvement.

**Focus:** Biostatistics with an emphasis on semiparametric methods, targeted learning, and causal inference. More methodological than clinical-trial focused.

**Why this is a natural fit for CTML students:** Editorial lineage traces directly through the targeted learning community. A very high fraction of the TMLE methodology literature has landed here, including collaborative TMLE, longitudinal TMLE extensions, and various sieve-based approaches.

**Representative work:**

- Van der Laan and collaborators' work on collaborative double-robust inference
- Extensions of TMLE to complex longitudinal settings

**Caveat:** Its impact factor is modest, which matters for external evaluators. Within the subfield, it is a respected venue. For tenure cases at Berkeley, this is typically well-understood; at other institutions, you may need to explain.

## Journal of Causal Inference

**What it is:** Dedicated journal for causal inference methodology.

**Focus:** Causal inference across fields — statistics, epidemiology, economics, computer science. Publishes work that might sit awkwardly between disciplinary homes.

**Representative work:** Pearl-tradition graphical causal inference, identification theorems, methodology that spans disciplines.
